did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep generative models (DCGAN, VAE, and DDPM) for data augmentation on the HAM10000 skin lesion dataset to address class imbalance, and evaluated the results using a ResNet50 deep learning classifier for skin lesion classification.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the application of deep generative models (DCGAN, VAE, DDPM) for data augmentation on the HAM10000 dataset and evaluates their performance using a ResNet50 classifier. It reports specific experimental results, metrics (accuracy, F1-score), and comparisons, which characterizes it as a research study rather than a review. Although published in a conference proceedings (IC3ECSBHI), it contains original results and methodology.

title: Balancing the HAM10000 Dataset Using Deep Generative Models: Evaluating GAN, VAE and DDPM for Skin Lesion Classification
journal: 2026 2nd International Conference on Cognitive Computing in Engineering, Communications, Sciences and Biomedical Health Informatics (IC3ECSBHI)
brief_summary: This study addresses class imbalance in the HAM10000 skin lesion dataset by employing three deep generative models—DCGAN, VAE, and DDPM—for data augmentation. The researchers expanded minority classes to 2,000 images each, creating a balanced dataset of 18,705 images to train a ResNet50 classifier. The performance of the augmented datasets was evaluated against a baseline using accuracy and macro F1-score metrics. The DDPM-based augmentation achieved the highest performance with 0.88 accuracy and 0.88 macro F1-score, outperforming DCGAN (0.87, 0.87) and VAE (0.86, 0.87). Notably, the DDPM method significantly improved classification for rare classes, achieving an F1 score of 0.99 for vascular lesions. The results demonstrate that diffusion-based augmentation effectively mitigates class imbalance and enhances the reliability of automated skin lesion diagnosis.

all_task_types.target: Seven discrete skin lesion classes (akiec, bcc, bkl, df, mel, nv, vasc)
all_task_types.details: The paper evaluates a ResNet50 classifier for skin lesion classification on the HAM10000 dataset. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (lesion types) using weighted cross-entropy loss. Performance is measured via accuracy and macro F1-score. The task is multiclass classification.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A large collection of multi-source dermatoscopic images of common pigmented skin lesions, widely used in dermatology research. The dataset contains 10,015 dermoscopic images representing seven types of skin lesion classes.

use.training: Used for training the baseline ResNet50 classifier (7,010 samples) and as the source for generative augmentation models (DCGAN, VAE, DDPM) to create synthetic samples for minority classes.
use.validation: Used for validation of the baseline ResNet50 classifier (1,503 samples).
use.testing: Used for testing the baseline ResNet50 classifier (1,503 samples) and evaluating the performance of classifiers trained on augmented data.
use.other: Used as the source dataset for generating synthetic images via DCGAN, VAE, and DDPM to balance class distribution. Minority classes were expanded to 2,000 images each, resulting in a balanced dataset of 18,705 images for classifier training.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons between the baseline and augmented models were made using accuracy and macro F1-score metrics without statistical significance testing.

HAM10000.experiment: ResNet50 Classification on Augmented vs Baseline Data
HAM10000.notes: DDPM achieved the best overall performance. Notably, for the vascular lesion (vasc) minority class, the DDPM-augmented model achieved an F1 score of 0.99. The paper did not report per-class metrics for all models in the text, only highlighting the vascular lesion improvement for DDPM. Quantitative evaluation of synthetic image quality (FID/Inception Score) was not performed.

architecture_used.pretraining: ResNet50 pretrained on ImageNet; Generative models (DCGAN, VAE, DDPM) trained from scratch on HAM10000 minority classes.
architecture_used.input_size: 128x128 for generative models; 224x224 for ResNet50 classifier.

DCGAN.generator: 4-layer architecture; converts 128-dim latent vector to 128x128 RGB images using transposed convolutions, batch normalization, and ReLU.
DCGAN.discriminator: 4-layer architecture; uses strided convolutions with LeakyReLU activations.

VAE.encoder: 4-layer design; compresses 128x128 image to 128-dim latent vector (mean and variance).
VAE.decoder: Reconstructs images using transposed convolutions, batch normalization, and ReLU.

DDPM.noise_schedule: Progressive Gaussian noise addition during training; reverse diffusion process for generation.

classifier.loss_function: Weighted Cross-Entropy Loss (weights derived from inverse class frequencies)

generative_models.VAE_loss: Weighted sum of MSE and KL divergence (beta=1e-3)

classification_or_output_head.ResNet50_head: Added fully connected layer outputting 7 class predictions.

preprocessing_techniques: Resizing images to 128x128 for training generative models (DCGAN, VAE, DDPM)
Resizing images to 224x224 for the ResNet50 classification stage
Normalization using standard ImageNet mean and standard deviation

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Used for per-class synthetic data generation on minority classes (akiec, bcc, bkl, df, mel, vasc). Each minority class was expanded to 2,000 images. Trained for 5 epochs with batch size 64, learning rate 2e-4, and latent dimension 128. Generator converts 128-dim latent vector to 128x128 RGB images using transposed convolutions, batch normalization, and ReLU. Discriminator uses strided convolutions with LeakyReLU. Loss function: BCEWithLogitsLoss.

Used for per-class synthetic data generation on minority classes. Each minority class was expanded to 2,000 images. Trained for 5 epochs with batch size 64, learning rate 2e-4, and latent dimension 128. Encoder compresses 128x128 image to 128-dim latent vector (mean and variance). Decoder reconstructs images using transposed convolutions, batch normalization, and ReLU. Loss function: Weighted sum of MSE and KL divergence (beta=1e-3).

Used for per-class synthetic data generation on minority classes. Each minority class was expanded to 2,000 images. Trained for 5 epochs with batch size 32, learning rate 2e-4, and latent dimension 128. Uses simplified U-Net architecture with 1,000 timesteps. Progressive Gaussian noise added during training; reverse diffusion process for generation. Loss function: MSE noise prediction loss.

Minority classes (akiec, bcc, bkl, df, mel, vasc) were expanded to exactly 2,000 images each using the generative models. The majority class (nv) retained its original 6,705 images. This resulted in a balanced dataset of 18,705 images total.

conclusions: DDPM-based data augmentation achieved the best overall performance for skin lesion classification on the HAM10000 dataset, reaching 0.88 accuracy and 0.88 macro F1-score, outperforming DCGAN (0.87, 0.87), VAE (0.86, 0.87), and the baseline model (0.80, 0.67).
Diffusion-based augmentation significantly improved classification performance for rare minority classes, specifically achieving an F1 score of 0.99 for vascular lesions (vasc).

Generative augmentation using DCGAN, VAE, and DDPM effectively mitigated class imbalance in the HAM10000 dataset, with the dominance of the majority class (nv) decreasing from 67% to approximately 36%.
The study demonstrates that deep generative models can create realistic synthetic samples that enhance the reliability of automated skin lesion diagnosis, particularly for rare skin lesions.

The current approach relies on a single image-only model, which is acknowledged as a limitation; future work will explore longer training, advanced diffusion models, and quantitative evaluation of synthetic image quality via FID or Inception Score.
important_remarks: The study lacks external validation; performance was evaluated solely on the HAM10000 test set without testing on independent datasets like ISIC-2019/2020.
Quantitative evaluation of synthetic image quality (e.g., FID or Inception Score) was not performed, relying only on downstream classifier metrics.
Training duration was limited to only 5 epochs for both generative models and the classifier, which may have constrained image quality and model convergence.
The approach relies exclusively on image-only data, ignoring clinical metadata (e.g., patient age, sex, location) which multimodal models often utilize for higher accuracy.
Signs of training instability were observed in DCGAN, including potential mode collapse (sharp loss increases for akiec and bkl) and adversarial spikes.
VAE training showed brief instability with validation loss spikes, suggesting potential overfitting or sensitivity to hyperparameters during the short training window.
DDPM sampling is noted as computationally expensive, which may limit deployment feasibility compared to GAN or VAE approaches.
The dataset remains multi-source with inherent demographic and geographic biases from the original HAM10000 collection, which are not addressed by the augmentation method.
No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) was reported to validate the performance differences between the augmentation methods.
The majority class (nv) was retained at its original count (6,705) while minority classes were artificially expanded to 2,000, creating a synthetic balance that may not reflect real-world prevalence.
